questions.question_text: The Colorado Contract to Buy and Sell Real Estate exists in how many versions?
questions.explanation: Colorado uses three separate versions of the Contract to Buy and Sell: Residential, Commercial, and Land. Each is tailored to its transaction type.

questions.question_text: Where are all contingency deadlines consolidated in the Colorado Contract to Buy and Sell?
questions.explanation: The Dates and Deadlines section is a critical feature of Colorado's contract — it consolidates all contingency deadlines in one location for easy reference.

questions.question_text: A buyer misses the appraisal deadline specified in the Dates and Deadlines section. This may constitute:
questions.explanation: Failure to meet a deadline specified in the Dates and Deadlines section may constitute a default under the contract. Deadlines in this section are enforceable.

questions.question_text: Under Colorado's Statute of Frauds, which agreement must be in writing to be enforceable?
questions.correct_answer: A contract to sell real property
distractors: A verbal agreement for a 6-month lease
An oral promise to pay a referral fee
A handshake deal for personal property
questions.explanation: The Statute of Frauds requires real estate sales contracts to be in writing and signed. A 6-month lease (under 1 year) may be oral.

questions.question_text: The Colorado Real Estate Commission updated its standard contract forms most recently in:
questions.explanation: The most recent major update to Commission-approved forms took effect August 15, 2024, incorporating changes from the NAR settlement and legislative updates.

questions.question_text: During the inspection period, a buyer finds unsatisfactory conditions. The buyer's first step is to submit:
distractors: A verbal complaint to the seller
A request for contract termination only
questions.explanation: The buyer submits a written Inspection Objection identifying unsatisfactory conditions. This begins the negotiation process for repairs, credits, or resolution.

questions.question_text: After the buyer submits an Inspection Objection, the parties enter the:
questions.explanation: After objection, the parties negotiate through the Inspection Resolution phase, working to agree on repairs, credits, or other remedies.

questions.question_text: A buyer decides during the inspection period that the roof condition is unacceptable. Without submitting an objection, the buyer can:
questions.correct_answer: Terminate the contract during the period
distractors: Only terminate after objection and resolution
questions.explanation: The buyer may terminate during the inspection period for any unsatisfactory condition. The Inspection Objection process is optional — the buyer can simply terminate.

questions.question_text: The Inspection Objection and Resolution process cannot be resolved. Under the contract, either party may:
distractors: Force the other party to comply
Escalate to the Real Estate Commission
questions.explanation: If the Inspection Objection and Resolution process fails, either party may terminate according to the contract terms. Neither party can force compliance.

questions.question_text: A buyer submits an Inspection Objection after the inspection deadline has passed. This objection is:
questions.correct_answer: Likely untimely and may be rejected
distractors: Automatically accepted as a late filing
Valid if the defect is serious enough
Extended by 5 days under Commission rules
questions.explanation: Deadlines in the Dates and Deadlines section are enforceable. Submitting an objection after the deadline has passed may constitute a late filing that the seller can reject.

questions.question_text: In Colorado, the amount of earnest money deposited is:
distractors: Set at 1% of the purchase price
questions.explanation: Earnest money amounts are freely negotiable between the parties. There is no fixed amount or percentage required by Colorado law.

questions.question_text: A buyer defaults on a Colorado purchase contract. The earnest money:
questions.correct_answer: May serve as liquidated damages per the contract
distractors: Is always returned to the buyer
Is automatically forfeited to the seller
Is held until a court orders disbursement
questions.explanation: The contract specifies the disposition of earnest money on buyer default, which may include liquidated damages. The exact treatment depends on the contract terms.

questions.question_text: Earnest money in Colorado is typically held by the:
questions.correct_answer: Listing broker's trust or title company
questions.explanation: Earnest money is typically held in the listing broker's trust account or by a title company, as specified in the contract.

questions.question_text: Both buyer and seller claim the earnest money after a transaction falls through. The broker should:
distractors: Split it evenly between the parties
Return it to the buyer automatically
questions.explanation: When there is a dispute over earnest money, the broker should follow the contract's dispute resolution provisions. Unilateral decisions by the broker are not appropriate.

questions.question_text: A buyer offers $500 in earnest money on a $400,000 home. The seller finds this insufficient. The seller may:
questions.correct_answer: Counter with a higher earnest money amount
distractors: Reject the offer and report the buyer
Accept only if buyer increases to 1%
Not negotiate the earnest money amount
questions.explanation: Earnest money is negotiable. The seller can counter with a request for more earnest money. There is no minimum amount required by law.

questions.question_text: Title insurance in Colorado transactions is:
questions.explanation: Title insurance is standard in Colorado real estate transactions, typically provided by a title insurance company. The contract specifies who pays for the owner's policy.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants broader title coverage than the standard owner's policy. They should obtain:
distractors: A second owner's policy from another company
A title guarantee from the Commission
A quitclaim deed from the seller
questions.explanation: The ALTA extended coverage policy provides broader protection than a standard policy. The buyer pays the additional cost for this enhanced coverage.

questions.question_text: The title insurance commitment is typically reviewed during which period?
distractors: Only if the buyer objects to it
questions.explanation: The title insurance commitment is reviewed during the due diligence period, allowing the buyer to identify and address any title issues before closing.

questions.question_text: Who pays for the owner's title insurance policy in Colorado?
distractors: Always the seller by state law
Always the buyer by state law
questions.explanation: The contract specifies who pays for the owner's title insurance policy. There is no state law requiring one party or the other to pay.

questions.question_text: A title commitment reveals an easement not previously disclosed. During due diligence, the buyer may:
distractors: Demand the seller remove the easement first
questions.explanation: Title issues discovered during due diligence can be objected to. The buyer can negotiate resolution with the seller or terminate if the issue cannot be resolved.
